model_narrative: We plan production over a sequence of periods to meet a known demand in each period. For each period we decide how much to produce, whether to set up production that period, and how much inventory to carry over to the next period. Setting up production in a period incurs a fixed setup cost, producing units incurs a per-unit production cost, and carrying inventory incurs a per-unit holding cost each period. The objective is to minimize the total cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the planning periods in chronological order; the first member is the opening period and each later member follows the one before it, and the last member is the final period of the horizon

params.name: setupcost
params.doc: the fixed cost incurred in any period in which production is set up, in dollars per period; a scalar

params.name: prodcost
params.doc: the variable cost of producing one unit, in dollars per unit; a scalar

params.name: invcost
params.doc: the base cost of holding one unit in inventory for one period, in dollars per unit per period; a scalar

params.name: stockini
params.doc: the inventory on hand at the very start of the horizon, before the first period, in units; a scalar

params.name: demand
params.doc: the demand that must be met in each period, in units

params.name: bigM
params.doc: a per-period upper bound on how much can be produced in that period; used as the large constant that links production to the setup decision, in units

params.name: invcoef
params.doc: the per-period inventory holding cost coefficient applied to ending inventory; equal to the base holding cost in every period except the final period of the horizon, where it is half the base holding cost, in dollars per unit per period

vars.name: s
vars.doc: the inventory held at the end of each period, in units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity produced in each period, in units

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the setup indicator for each period; equals 1 if production is set up in that period and 0 otherwise

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total cost over the whole horizon, in dollars; a scalar

constraints.name: x[t]
constraints.intent: Production can only happen in a period when that period has been set up for production. For each period the amount produced must not exceed that period's production upper bound when the period is set up, and must be zero when the period is not set up.

constraints.name: t)
constraints.intent: Inventory must be conserved from one period to the next. For each period, the stock carried in from the previous period together with what is produced that period must cover that period's demand and leave the ending inventory for that period. In the very first period there is no previous period, so the starting inventory on hand at the beginning of the horizon plays that role instead.

constraints.name: cost
constraints.intent: The total cost gathers the holding, setup, and production charges across the whole horizon. For every period, charge the holding cost on that period's ending inventory, charge the fixed setup cost whenever that period is set up for production, and charge the per-unit production cost on what is produced that period. Set the total cost equal to the sum of all these amounts over every period.

query: There's a proposal on the table to lean harder on our two big consolidation runs instead of spreading volume around. Suppose we require the first-period and third-period batches together to put out at least 2400 units between them — what does the optimizer return for the total cost?

query: We're looking at a front-loading rule for working capital: the stock we carry out of period 1 should be no smaller than the stock we carry out of period 3, so the cushion sits early rather than mid-horizon. Plug that in and tell me where the total cost lands.

query: Maintenance is pitching a setup budget — they only want the line spun up for a fresh production run in three periods across the whole horizon, not six. If we hold the number of periods that fire a setup to at most three, what total cost does the plan come back with?
expected_pyomo: model.setup_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[t] for t in model.t) <= 3)

query: The plan keeps running periods bone-dry, leaving us with zero cushion if a delivery slips. Shouldn't every period through the back half close out holding at least 150 units of safety stock instead of running it down to nothing? What's the model seeing that makes carrying no buffer the better call?

query: That period-3 batch of 1600 units is a monster, and it only works because the plan leans on a free period-4 to mop up behind it. Couldn't we split it — hold the t3 run to 800 and forbid spinning the line up again in t4 — and cover demand more evenly? What's the tradeoff keeping the model from doing that?

query: Some of these batches are enormous — the t3 run alone is 1600 units, which slams the line in a single go and leaves us no flexibility. Wouldn't it be cleaner to put a uniform ceiling on every period's run, say a thousand units apiece, and spread the work out? What's pulling the optimizer toward those oversized lots?

query: We've committed nearly the whole second half of the horizon to back-to-back fresh runs. If demand is concentrated there anyway, couldn't we lean on the line and have periods five through eight crank out at least 5000 units between them rather than dribbling production out? What's keeping the model from front-loading that back-half volume?
